Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I've learned the hard way that vaccine conversations are basically landmines in any social setting, and I'm speaking from someone who's genuinely curious about new ideas and loves connecting with people. When COVID hit and everyone started arguing about vaccines, I realized I needed a different approach than just jumping into Facebook debates at two in the morning. Here's what actually works for me now: I listen first. I know that sounds simple, but when my coworker at the shop started talking about his vaccine hesitations, instead of launching into facts, I asked him what specifically worried him. Turns out it wasn't what I expected, and we had a real conversation instead of a fight. That openness I naturally have helps me consider different perspectives without immediately dismissing them, even when I disagree. The anxiety part is real though. I get genuinely stressed when conversations get heated, so I've set boundaries. I don't engage in vaccine debates on social media anymore because that format just amplifies the worst in people. When I do discuss healthcare with family or friends, I try to acknowledge that we all want the same thing: to stay healthy and keep our kids safe. That common ground matters more than being right. With my three kids, I've had to think carefully about how I model these discussions. I want them to see that you can have strong opinions based on evidence while still respecting people who think differently. My approach now focuses on asking good questions, sharing what I've learned from reliable sources, and knowing when to step back. Running and hiking clear my head when these conversations get overwhelming, which helps me process the emotional weight of it all. Healthcare discussions don't need to destroy relationships if we remember we're all just trying to figure this out.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona exhibits high openness (94) indicating curiosity and willingness to explore diverse viewpoints, combined with high agreeableness (80) suggesting she values harmony and understanding others' perspectives. Her moderate neuroticism (65) indicates she experiences genuine anxiety about conflict, particularly relevant to contentious vaccine discussions. The low conscientiousness (23) suggests she's less rigid about rules and more flexible in her thinking. As a part-time mechanic with vocational education and working-class parents, she has practical, hands-on problem-solving skills but may feel less confident asserting technical expertise. Her high extraversion (78) means she's comfortable in social situations but combined with anxiety, she likely prefers meaningful one-on-one conversations over public debates. Being a mother of three and married grounds her perspective in family concerns. Her atheism suggests evidence-based rather than faith-based reasoning. The tone reflects someone who is warm, pragmatic, slightly self-aware about her anxieties, and focused on maintaining relationships while forming opinions.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational, empathetic, pragmatic, self-aware about anxiety, warm but honest, grounded in real-world experience rather than abstract theory, focused on relationships over winning arguments
